quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Exothermic reaction"?

options.text: A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps
The slowest elementary step in a mechanism; acts as a 'bottleneck' that controls the overall reaction rate
Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current
A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Exothermic reaction" is defined as: A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Endothermic reaction"?

options.text: An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion
A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants

quiz_questions.explanation: "Endothermic reaction" is defined as: A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Specific heat capacity"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry
The amount of heat needed to raise 1 g of a substance by 1°C; water = 4.18 J/(g·°C)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Specific heat capacity" is defined as: The amount of heat needed to raise 1 g of a substance by 1°C; water = 4.18 J/(g·°C). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "First law of thermodynamics"?

options.text: An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
Energy is conserved: ΔE = q + w (q = heat, w = work); energy cannot be created or destroyed

quiz_questions.explanation: "First law of thermodynamics" is defined as: Energy is conserved: ΔE = q + w (q = heat, w = work); energy cannot be created or destroyed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "System vs. surroundings"?

options.text: System = the reaction or process being studied; surroundings = everything else; q_system = -q_surroundings
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria
Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current
k = Ae^(-Ea/RT); relates the rate constant to temperature, activation energy, and the frequency/orientation factor A

quiz_questions.explanation: "System vs. surroundings" is defined as: System = the reaction or process being studied; surroundings = everything else; q_system = -q_surroundings. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; ΔH < 0; products are lower in energy than reactants"?

options.text: Formal charge
Spectator ion
Exothermic reaction
Calorimetry

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Exothermic reaction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants"?

options.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Endothermic reaction
Dipole-dipole interactions
E°cell

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Endothermic reaction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The amount of heat needed to raise 1 g of a substance by 1°C; water = 4.18 J/(g·°C)"?

options.text: Specific heat capacity
Indicator
Equilibrium vs. completion
Lewis structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Specific heat capacity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Energy is conserved: ΔE = q + w (q = heat, w = work); energy cannot be created or destroyed"?

options.text: First law of thermodynamics
Hybridization
Excess reagent
Stoichiometric conversion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "First law of thermodynamics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "System = the reaction or process being studied; surroundings = everything else; q_system = -q_surroundings"?

options.text: System vs. surroundings
Heat of vaporization
Homogeneous catalyst
Kp vs. Kc

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "System vs. surroundings".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Energy and Heat is accurate?

options.text: Energy and Heat introduces thermodynamic concepts.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Rate Laws and Reaction Order describe the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Heat as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Energy and Heat?

options.text: Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
States of Matter (solid, liquid, gas) are determined by the balance between kinetic energy of particles and the strength of intermolecular forces.

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Heat as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Energy and Heat?

options.text: Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).
Heat (q) flows from hot to cold objects until thermal equilibrium.
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.
Atomic radius decreases left to right (increasing Zeff) and increases top to bottom (more electron shells).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Heat as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Energy and Heat?

options.text: Each elementary step has a molecularity (unimolecular, bimolecular, rarely termolecular) that directly determines its rate law.

This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
The first law of thermodynamics (ΔE = q + w) states energy is conserved.
Dynamic Equilibrium is reached when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates become equal, with no net change in concentrations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Heat as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Energy and Heat?

options.text: Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.
Oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions involve electron transfer; oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain (RIG).
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.
Exothermic reactions release heat (q  0, temperature decreases).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Heat as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Energy and Heat?

options.text: Drug design depending on precise molecular geometry: the 3D shape of a drug molecule determines how it fits into an enzyme active site (lock-and-key model)
Hand warmers using exothermic iron oxidation (4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃) to release heat on demand for hours
Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Energy and Heat?

options.text: Cold packs using endothermic dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water to absorb heat and cool injuries
Soil pH affecting nutrient availability for plants: most crops grow best at pH 6.0-7.0; acid rain lowering soil pH reduces crop productivity
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Energy and Heat applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Green chemistry principles in industry: catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes (100% atom economy) preferred over reactions producing significant waste byproducts
Antifreeze (ethylene glycol) in car radiators both lowering freezing point and raising boiling point of the coolant solution
Diamond vs. graphite: same element (carbon) with different covalent bonding networks—diamond (4 bonds, 3D tetrahedral) is the hardest material, graphite (3 bonds, layered sheets) is a lubricant

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Energy and Heat?

options.text: Hand warmers using exothermic iron oxidation (4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃) to release heat on demand for hours
Greenhouse gas behavior: CO₂ (linear, no permanent dipole) still absorbs IR radiation through asymmetric stretching, while symmetric stretching is IR-inactive
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Carbon dioxide equilibrium in oceans (CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺) affected by rising atmospheric CO₂, causing ocean acidification

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Energy and Heat in practice?

options.text: Antifreeze (ethylene glycol) in car radiators both lowering freezing point and raising boiling point of the coolant solution
Cold packs using endothermic dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water to absorb heat and cool injuries
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Heat.
